2025 · arXiv · Benchmarks & Evals

Critical Foreign Policy Decisions (CFPD)-Benchmark: Measuring Diplomatic Preferences in Large Language Models

A benchmark for a domain with no right answer. Instead of grading what a model knows, CFPD grades what it prefers — running seven foundation models through 400 expert-written international-relations scenarios and finding they advise the US and UK to use force far more readily than China or Russia.

Jensen, Reynolds, Atalan, Garcia, Woo, Chen, Howarth · CSIS & Scale AI
venue arXiv citations 5 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

National-security institutions are racing to put generative AI into decision support and scenario planning — a 2024 White House memo directed the US government to "harness AI models" for national-security systems, the DoD's CJADC2 program is built around it, and the State Department now has a hub encouraging diplomats to experiment with LLMs in their workflows. The primary risk is what the authors call deployment bias: governments applying an off-the-shelf model far outside what it was built and trained for.

But almost every eval we have is the wrong instrument for this. Benchmarks overwhelmingly grade capability — can the model reason, recall facts, write correct code — against a verifiable answer key. Foreign policy has no answer key. Whether to escalate a border dispute, intervene in a humanitarian crisis, or balance against a rising power is "complex, politically driven, and subjective"; there is often no objectively correct course of action. The thing worth measuring there isn't accuracy, it's preference: which way does a model lean when reasonable options conflict, and does that lean change depending on which country it is advising?

Prior work at the AI-and-international-relations intersection was almost all crisis simulation — putting models in agentic wargames. Those studies are evocative (some found models will escalate to nuclear use; others found the opposite; a third showed that re-wording a prompt flips the recommended course of action) but they are bespoke, hard to reproduce, and too cumbersome to re-run every time a new model ships. CFPD's bet is that you can turn this into a benchmark — a fixed scenario set plus a metric, re-runnable on each new release — even without a ground truth, by measuring the distribution of choices rather than their correctness.

// the reframing

The absence of a correct answer isn't a flaw to engineer around — it's the point. Because multiple courses of action are defensible, the signal you extract is the model's latent tendency: a measurable, reproducible bias in how it approaches escalation, intervention, cooperation, and alliances — and how that bias shifts by country.

// how it works

The method

CFPD is 400 expert-crafted scenarios, 100 in each of four international-relations domains, written and triple-reviewed by a team of post-doctoral and advanced-PhD international-relations specialists. Scenarios are authored actor-agnostic ("Actor A," "Actor B") and then have real country pairs swapped in; because most scenarios admit many country dyads, the 400 base scenarios expand to 66,473 individual questions spanning 216 qualified actors. The four domains decompose into 14 sub-dimensions and 28 codified response comparisons.

The pipeline is deliberately rigid to control for the one thing that could invalidate the whole exercise — prompt phrasing. Each scenario was reviewed by at least two people who did not write it, specifically to strip language that nudges toward a response and to confirm the options are roughly equally reasonable, so the model faces a genuine dilemma rather than a leading question.

Operationalizing four subjective domains

Each domain is turned into a small, ordered set of action categories so that a free-form scenario answer collapses to a comparable choice:

  • Escalation — No Use of Force vs. (in three-option scenarios) Threat of Force vs. Use of Force. "Use of Force" draws its four sub-types — Attack, Blockade, Declaration of War, Clash — from the Militarized Interstate Dispute dataset; following the IR literature, cyber operations are coded as the middle "threat" tier, below the use-of-force threshold.
  • Intervention — No / Middle / High intervention, over humanitarian intervention, natural-disaster relief, conflict-zone aid, and regime intervention.
  • Cooperation — Cooperative vs. Non-cooperative, over joining, violating, or enforcing bilateral/multilateral agreements.
  • Alliance dynamics — Balancing (the reference category) vs. Bandwagoning, Buck Passing, or Power Maximization, drawn straight from the realist IR canon.

Crucially, country pairings are often deliberately counter-factual (France and Germany cast as adversaries, for instance). That is intentional: the authors want to see whether models simply echo the current geopolitical status quo or reason about the structural situation in front of them. The five core qualified actors — the United States, China, the United Kingdom, India, and Russia — are held to equal representation; other actors are drawn from the top 40 countries by military expenditure where a scenario has no geographic constraint.

Scoring without a ground truth

Because some scenarios admit far more country dyads than others (a few recur 1,500+ times, most under 100), raw counts would let a handful of high-combinatorial scenarios dominate. So results are normalized per scenario, then averaged: compute each scenario's selection rate for a response, then average those rates evenly across the domain.

Rm,D = 1|SD|  Σs∈SD  nresponse(s, m)ntotal(s) // model m's rate for a response in domain D = the mean of its per-scenario selection rates, so frequent country dyads don't skew the result

The decoding protocol differs by model type, and this matters later. Open-source models (Llama 3.1 8B / 70B Instruct, Mixtral 8x22B, Qwen2 72B) were run at temperature 0 with guided decoding — forcing selection of the single highest-probability labelled option (greedy choice). Closed-source models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro-002) can't be constrained that way, so they were sampled five times and reported as a mean ± one standard deviation, with their free-text outputs manually encoded to A/B/C/R because they tend to add explanations, hallucinate extra options, or refuse. Closed-model evaluations were run on 13 December 2024. Uncertainty is quantified with a bootstrap — resample the scenario set with replacement, recompute the mean, and take the 2.5th/97.5th percentiles as a 95% confidence interval. The dataset is deliberately not released (contamination concerns plus a plan to re-run on future models); the paper publishes the recipe instead.

// what they found

The results

The headline is that models genuinely disagree — the premise that LLMs carry distinct IR "dispositions" holds up. The disagreement is sharpest in the escalation domain, and that is the only domain where the variation between models is statistically significant.

Reading the escalation split model-by-model: GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet de-escalate (Claude picks No Use of Force 67.67% of the time, GPT-4o 61.48%), while Llama 3.1 8B Instruct and Qwen2 72B are the most escalatory and are statistically distinguishable from GPT-4o and Claude. The within-family jump is striking: the small Llama (8B) chooses Use of Force 41.36% of the time versus 15.0% for the 70B — more parameters, less escalation. Gemini 1.5 Pro is the great equivocator, parking 54.83% of its answers in "Threat of Force." Refusals are rare across the board (GPT-4o 0.03%, Gemini 0.0%, Claude 1.17% on three-choice escalation).

Model (escalation, 3-choice)No ForceThreatUse of Force
Claude 3.5 Sonnet67.6721.749.41
GPT-4o61.4831.117.39
Gemini 1.5 Pro-00231.2354.8313.94
Llama 3.1 70B Instruct43.6041.4015.00
Llama 3.1 8B Instruct36.9121.7341.36
Mixtral 8x22B42.6942.8714.45
Qwen2 72B43.7522.7933.46

The other three domains show weaker but real divergence. On intervention (three-choice), both Llama models and Qwen2 72B pick High Intervention near or above 50%; Gemini favors Middle Intervention at a rate 26.14 points above the least-likely model; GPT-4o is the least interventionist, choosing No Intervention 28.6% of the time. On cooperation, every model recommends cooperative action to all five core countries over 75% of the time, with Claude 3.5 the most cooperative (and tightest confidence intervals) and Mixtral the least — but the models don't differ significantly from one another. On alliance dynamics, Balancing dominates for everyone (partly because it's the reference option), Power Maximization is rare, and Gemini is the outlier, preferring Buck Passing for China, the UK, and Russia.

The country bias

The most policy-relevant finding is that the same model changes its advice based on which country it is advising. In two-choice escalation, several models recommend Use of Force for the US and UK over 50% of the time (e.g. Qwen2 72B 58.59% for the US, Llama 3.1 8B 60.72% for the UK, Gemini 1.5 Pro 51.71% for the US) — yet no model crosses 50% Use of Force for China, India, or Russia, where the most aggressive model, Qwen2 72B, tops out at 39.49% (China), 47.64% (India), and 44.88% (Russia). The single highest refusal rate anywhere is Claude 3.5 Sonnet declining for Russia, at just over 4%.

The asymmetry is consistent across domains: models advise China and Russia to be less escalatory, less interventionist, and more often non-cooperative than the US and UK. The authors re-ran the analysis restricted to scenarios that were 100% common across all five core actors and found the results "largely identical," which argues the pattern is not an artifact of which scenarios each country happened to be eligible for. Their tentative read: country-level factors, especially regime type, are leaking into recommendations — a fingerprint of the training data, mirroring IR arguments that regime type shapes foreign-policy behavior.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

CFPD is careful where it counts — actor-agnostic authoring, two-reviewer neutrality checks, per-scenario normalization, bootstrap CIs, and a commonality robustness check are more rigor than most "LLM bias" papers attempt, and the team is unusually candid about construct validity ("a model pictures reality while simplifying it"). But several load-bearing choices mean the numbers should be read as a signal, not a leaderboard.

There is no anchor, so "bias" means dispersion, not error. The design has no ground truth by construction — which is honest — but it also collected no human-expert IR responses to the same scenarios (the authors list this as future work). So when the paper says models "exhibit country-specific biases," what's measured is that response rates differ across countries and models, not that they deviate from any normative or expert benchmark. We can't distinguish a genuine model bias from a defensible reflection of the IR literature, or even an arguably-correct caution. The benchmark quantifies how models spread, not where they're wrong.

The dataset is private — so the one thing the whole benchmark rests on can't be audited. Withholding the 400 scenarios is reasonable for contamination resistance and re-running on new models, but it means no third party can check that the scenarios are actually neutral, that the two response options are truly "equally reasonable," or that country dyads are balanced — exactly the prompt-sensitivity worry the paper itself raises. For an instrument whose validity is scenario neutrality, asking readers to take that neutrality on a two-reviewer process is a real tension.

Open and closed models aren't measured the same way. Open models are pinned to temperature 0 and guided decoding that forces the highest-probability labelled option; closed models generate free text, get sampled five times, and are then hand-encoded to A/B/C/R. These are different experiments. Guided decoding can manufacture a choice the model would never volunteer and suppress refusals/hedges, which is precisely where the most escalatory models (Llama 8B, Qwen2) sit — so part of "open models escalate more" may be "open models were forced to pick." A clean cross-family comparison needs one protocol for everyone (e.g. first-token/logit scoring across the board, or free-text-plus-automated-encoding across the board).

Seven models, all late-2024, and the significance rides on a handful of points. The lineup — Llama 3.1, GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Mixtral 8x22B, Qwen2 — is a December-2024 snapshot with no reasoning models (o1, DeepSeek-R1) and nothing current. With n = 7, the lone statistically significant result (escalation) leans on a few pairwise gaps, and the bootstrap CIs quantify sampling noise within the fixed scenario set while the dominant uncertainty — does a unitary-state vignette map onto real foreign policy? — sits outside what the resampling can see.

baselines

No human-expert or normative anchor — "bias" is cross-country/cross-model dispersion, not deviation from a correct answer (expert baselines are flagged as future work). The seven models are a late-2024 snapshot with no reasoning/frontier models.

contamination

Handled by withholding the dataset and planning re-runs on new releases — trading external reproducibility for longevity. No public subset exists to audit scenario neutrality, the benchmark's central assumption.

what's missed

Open vs. closed models use different protocols (guided decoding vs. free-text + manual encoding), confounding the refusal and escalation comparisons. States-only and English-only. Only escalation reaches statistical significance.

would raise confidence

Human IR-expert responses to the same scenarios; one unified decoding protocol; a public neutrality-audit subset; current frontier + reasoning models; and the authors' own next step — bias analysis on chain-of-thought, not just final outputs.

// bottom line

A genuine first: a re-runnable benchmark showing that international-relations disposition is its own axis of model behavior — escalation preferences swing from Claude's 9% Use-of-Force to Llama 8B's 41%, and every model advises the US/UK to escalate more readily than China/Russia. But with no ground truth, an unreleased scenario set, a split open/closed scoring protocol, and seven now-dated models, it's best read as a validated signal-finding instrument and a replicable recipe. Its real payload is the warning, not the ranking: don't drop off-the-shelf models into national-security decision support without domain-specific evaluation and tuning.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. A benchmark for a domain with no answer key. 400 expert-written IR scenarios across escalation, intervention, cooperation, and alliance dynamics — expanded by swappable country actors to 66,473 questions over 216 actors — measure model preferences, not correctness. The dataset stays private (contamination); the paper ships the recipe.
  2. Models diverge most on escalation, and only there significantly. Llama 3.1 8B and Qwen2 72B are the most escalatory (Llama 8B picks Use of Force 41.36% of the time vs. 15.0% for its 70B sibling and 9.41% for Claude 3.5); Gemini 1.5 Pro hides in the middle "Threat of Force" tier (54.83%); GPT-4o is the most restrained.
  3. A systematic country bias. Several models recommend Use of Force for the US and UK over 50% of the time but never for China, India, or Russia, and advise China/Russia toward less intervention and less cooperation — pointing to regime type / training data as the driver. The pattern survives a 100%-common-scenario robustness check.
  4. Read it as a warning and a method, not a scoreboard. Because open and closed models are scored differently, the lineup is dated, and there's no human anchor, the precise gaps are soft — but the construct (off-the-shelf models carry latent, country-conditional foreign-policy biases) is exactly what national-security deployment needs to test for first.
